design_philosophy.core_loop: Throw detachable head to defeat enemies, grab hooks to navigate platforms, collect power-up heads that alter gameplay mechanics and abilities
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing health via spotlight meter; balancing offensive head attacks with navigational head-grabbing; adapting to temporary power-up heads with different properties
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Control a clever puppet protagonist with a detachable head as both weapon and tool; experience creative, visually inventive combat and platforming inspired by Western animation and Terry Gilliam's surrealist aesthetic
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game prioritizes creative originality and technical visual showcase over genre conventions. Rejects instant-death pit falls (Headdy bounces back with damage instead). Emphasizes head-swapping power-up system as core mechanic rather than traditional power-ups. Includes support characters in every stage rather than lone-hero formula. Heavily influenced by Western animation style, distinguishing it aesthetically from typical 16-bit platformers

difficulty.lives_system: Health represented by spotlight meter in corner; loses life if meter depletes without picking up restorative items
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression structure; no documented mid-stage checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Regional difficulty split: Japanese version designed for commercial viability; North American version made approximately twice as difficult per Sega of America request to prevent easy rentals. Players can manage difficulty through resource collection (health restoratives, extra lives scattered throughout levels)

progression.upgrade_system: 17 different interchangeable power-up heads with varying effects: damage increase (spinning fireballs), invincibility, health restoration, speed boost, etc. Liberty Head unlocks bonus stage. Power-ups range from permanent to temporary effects
progression.unlock_mechanism: Discover power-up heads scattered throughout stages; Liberty Head opens bonus basketball shooting stage
replayability_hooks: Multiple power-up head variants encourage different playstyle approaches
Bonus stage accessible via Liberty Head discovery
Support character appearances in each stage provide narrative texture
Regional version differences (Japan vs NA) create distinct difficulty experiences

world_building_techniques: Support characters appearing in every stage establish puppet world inhabited by allies
Evil puppet King Dark Demon as antagonist transforming world puppets into minions
Stage bosses (Trouble Bruin, Key Masters) represent King's army hierarchy
Dialogue present in Japanese version, significantly reduced in North American localization

boss_design.philosophy: Stage bosses (Key Masters and Trouble Bruin identified) serve as gatekeepers within King Dark Demon's hierarchy; designed as set encounters within linear progression rather than dynamic adaptive systems

faction_indicators: Evil puppet King Dark Demon commanding transformed minions
Support characters aligned with player (appearing in every stage)
Trouble Bruin and Key Masters as enemy hierarchy
danger_signals: King Dark Demon's minion-transforming power represents existential puppet world threat
Enemy encounters escalate through stage progression
Health depletion results in life loss, raising execution stakes
environmental_storytelling: Puppet world setting establishes whimsical yet threatened civilization
Western animation-inspired visual design creates cohesive world aesthetic
Terry Gilliam influence suggests surrealist, potentially anarchic worldbuilding
Platform navigation through multi-stage structure implies exploration of King's domain

sources_used: wikipedia - Gameplay section details mechanics, health system, 17 power-ups, Liberty Head bonus stage, pit bounce mechanic

wikipedia - Development and release section documents design philosophy (Kimura's vision of puppet with detachable head, support characters, Western animation/Terry Gilliam inspiration), regional difficulty split (NA version twice as difficult per Sega of America), dialogue removal in localization
wikipedia - notes Treasure as developer, Sega as publisher, August 5 1994 release
